title: Lean, Six Sigma & Continuous Improvement
summary: The main process-improvement approaches and where each one fits on a PMBOK 8 exam, broken into scan-friendly blocks: the continuous-improvement mindset and PDCA loop, the DMAIC and DMADV cycles, the 8 wastes (DOWNTIME), Lean vs Six Sigma, Lean Six Sigma belts, Kaizen and Theory of Constraints, and cues for matching the method to the symptom.
intro: Cut waste and variation with DMAIC, PDCA and the eight wastes.

sections.heading: Continuous improvement as a mindset
sections.body: PMBOK 8 treats quality and improvement as ongoing work, not a one-off phase. Continuous improvement supports the Embed Quality principle (embed quality into processes and deliverables) and sits within the Governance domain, where continuous improvement and quality assurance live, using reliable data to drive timely adjustments. Small, regular gains compound and beat occasional big pushes, so teams build improvement into the normal flow of delivery.

items.title: Plan
lines: Plan a change against expectations.

items.title: Do
lines: Do it on a small scale.

items.title: Check
lines: Check the result against what you expected.

items.title: Act
lines: Standardise what worked or adjust what did not, then repeat.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: If a question rewards steady, iterative refinement over a single large intervention, that is the continuous-improvement mindset.
Continuous improvement maps to the Embed Quality principle and the Governance domain.

sections.heading: Lean: maximise value, eliminate waste
sections.body: Lean comes from the Toyota Production System. Its aim is to maximise customer value while removing waste (muda) - anything the customer would not pay for.

items.text: Defects - errors and rework the customer would not pay for.
Overproduction - making more or sooner than demand needs.
Non-used talent - the eighth, people-focused waste; under-used skills.
Inventory - work or stock held beyond what is needed.
Extra-processing - over-processing beyond what adds value.

rows.value: Making more or sooner than needed.

blocks.title: Core Lean tools and ideas
lines: Value stream mapping to see the whole flow.
Creating smooth flow.
Using pull so work is drawn by demand.
Takt time to pace output to customer demand.

blocks.title: Memory aid
lines: DOWNTIME covers all 8 wastes; TIMWOOD is the classic 7 plus the eighth - under-used talent.

sections.heading: Six Sigma: reduce variation and defects
sections.body: Six Sigma uses data and statistics to reduce variation and defects. Its symbolic goal is around 3.4 defects per million opportunities, and it is driven by the voice of the customer - what the customer actually defines as a defect.

figure.caption: DMAIC: the Six Sigma cycle for improving an existing process.

items.title: Define
lines: Define the problem and the voice of the customer.

items.title: Measure
lines: Measure current performance with data.

items.title: Analyse
lines: Analyse to find root causes of variation.

items.title: Improve
lines: Improve the process to remove the causes.

items.title: Control
lines: Control to sustain the gains, then repeat.

blocks.title: DMADV - design a new process or product

items.title: Define
lines: Define design goals.

items.title: Measure
lines: Measure critical-to-quality needs.

items.title: Analyse
lines: Analyse design options.

items.title: Design
lines: Design the new process or product.

items.title: Verify
lines: Verify the design performs as intended.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: DMAIC improves what exists; DMADV designs something new.
The M-A split (Improve/Control vs Design/Verify) is the giveaway.

sections.heading: Lean Six Sigma
sections.body: Lean Six Sigma combines Lean's focus on waste and speed with Six Sigma's focus on variation and quality, so teams make processes both faster and more consistent.

groups.label: Focus
lines: Maximise value, eliminate waste.

groups.label: Attacks
lines: Speed and flow problems.
The 8 wastes (DOWNTIME).

groups.label: Tools
lines: Value stream mapping, pull, takt time.

groups.label: Focus
lines: Reduce variation and defects.

groups.label: Attacks
lines: Variation and defect problems where data exists.
Toward 3.4 defects per million.

groups.label: Tools
lines: DMAIC, DMADV, voice of the customer.

rows.value: Leads major projects and coaches others full-time.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: Lean attacks speed and waste; Six Sigma attacks variation - Lean Six Sigma does both at once.

sections.heading: Kaizen and Theory of Constraints
sections.body: Two more improvement angles that show up on the exam: an everyday-culture approach and a bottleneck-first approach.

groups.label: What it is
lines: Continuous small improvements made by everyone, every day, as part of normal work.

groups.label: Variant
lines: A Kaizen event - a short, intensive burst aimed at one process or area.

groups.label: What it is
lines: A system is limited by its single biggest bottleneck.

groups.label: How
lines: Find that constraint, exploit it, and subordinate everything else to it before improving elsewhere.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: Kaizen is a culture of many small wins; TOC says fix the one bottleneck first.

sections.heading: When each approach fits
sections.body: Tailor to context - these approaches are complementary, and the right answer depends on the symptom the question describes.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: Read for the symptom - waste and flow point to Lean, inconsistency and defects point to Six Sigma.
As always, tailor to context; the approaches are complementary.

sections.heading: Lean software principles for the exam
sections.body:
Lean software development adapts Lean thinking to knowledge work. The PMP exam may frame it as an agile or hybrid way to improve flow, reduce rework, and learn quickly while still protecting customer value.

The key idea is not speed at any cost. Lean asks the team to deliver useful increments, expose problems early, and remove activities that do not help the customer or the project outcome.
sections.tip: If an answer improves speed by skipping quality, stakeholder feedback, or team learning, it is usually not the best Lean answer.

items.text: Remove work that does not add value, such as unused features, avoidable handoffs, waiting, defects, or excessive work in progress.
Prevent defects through disciplined practices instead of relying on late inspection.
Use feedback, experiments, retrospectives, and shared learning to improve decisions.
Keep options open until enough information exists to make a responsible decision.
Shorten feedback cycles by delivering small, valuable increments.
Trust the people closest to the work and create conditions for ownership, safety, and improvement.
Improve the end-to-end system instead of making one activity look efficient at the expense of overall value.

sections.heading: Learning loops and knowledge creation
sections.body:
Continuous improvement depends on learning from real evidence. Short iterations, frequent stakeholder review, demonstrations, retrospectives, experiments, and lessons learned all help the team replace assumptions with knowledge.

For the exam, connect learning loops to risk reduction. The earlier the team validates a product decision, process change, or technical assumption, the less costly it is to adjust.

items.title: Deliver a small increment
lines: Keep the batch size small enough to inspect quickly.
Make the result visible to users, sponsors, or the team.

items.title: Gather feedback
lines: Look for evidence about value, defects, usability, risk, and flow.
Use data and observation, not only opinions.

items.title: Adapt the work system
lines: Update the backlog, process, quality practices, or decision assumptions.
Share what was learned so knowledge does not remain with one person.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: In agile and Lean scenarios, the project manager or servant leader should help the team learn, remove obstacles, and make information visible rather than dictate detailed solutions.

sections.heading: Build quality into the flow
sections.body:
Lean treats defects as waste because they create rework, delays, inspection effort, and loss of trust. Building quality in means designing the process so problems are prevented or found quickly while they are still inexpensive to correct.

This aligns with PMBOK quality management: plan quality, manage quality through the process, and control quality through inspection and measurement. Lean shifts the emphasis toward prevention and shared responsibility.

blocks.title: Quality built in versus inspected later

groups.label: What
lines: Quality practices are part of daily work.

groups.label: Use
lines: Peer review, checklists, automated tests, clear acceptance criteria, definition of done, and early validation.

groups.label: What
lines: Defects are found after significant work is complete.

groups.label: Risk
lines: Rework increases cost, schedule pressure, and stakeholder dissatisfaction.

sections.heading: Last responsible moment decisions
sections.body:
Defer commitment does not mean avoid decisions. It means delaying irreversible or costly choices until the team has enough information to decide responsibly.

This is useful when requirements, technology, market needs, or constraints are uncertain. The team preserves options, runs small experiments, and narrows choices as evidence improves.
sections.tip: On the exam, choose defer commitment when the problem is uncertainty. Do not choose it when a decision is urgent, risk exposure is increasing, or delay would block the team.

rows.value: The decision is reversible, needed to unblock work, or enough facts are already available.
More information is expected soon and waiting does not create meaningful risk.
The team can test an assumption with a prototype, spike, pilot, or small release.
The decision crosses authority boundaries, affects major constraints, or needs stakeholder alignment.

sections.heading: Fast delivery without overloading the team
sections.body:
Deliver fast means shortening the path from idea to feedback. It is achieved through small batches, clear priorities, limited work in progress, fewer handoffs, automation where useful, and rapid review.

Fast delivery is not the same as pushing people harder. Overloaded teams create queues, defects, context switching, and burnout. Lean improves flow by reducing friction in the system.

groups.label: What
lines: Break work into increments that can be completed and reviewed quickly.

groups.label: Use
lines: Reduce waiting time and make feedback faster.

groups.label: What
lines: Control the amount of started but unfinished work.

groups.label: Use
lines: Expose bottlenecks and reduce multitasking.

groups.label: What
lines: Address delays caused by dependencies, approvals, unclear requirements, or resource constraints.

groups.label: Use
lines: Help work move through the whole system, not just one step.

sections.heading: Respect people and optimize the whole
sections.body:
Lean improvement depends on the people doing the work. Respect people means listening to team insight, making constraints visible, encouraging problem solving, and giving teams the authority needed to improve their process.

Optimize the whole means avoiding local improvements that damage overall outcomes. A single department may look efficient while the project still suffers from delays, rework, or poor value delivery.

rows.value: Coach, facilitate, remove impediments, involve the team, and support psychological safety.
Assign solutions without team input, ignore work constraints, or blame individuals for system problems.
Improve end-to-end value delivery, including dependencies, queues, quality, and stakeholder feedback.
Maximize one metric or team output while increasing delays or rework elsewhere.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: When a scenario shows a bottleneck or cross-team delay, the best answer usually improves system flow and collaboration instead of pressuring one group to work faster.

source: PM Cram study notes - reworded for the current ECO-based PMP exam and aligned to PMBOK 8 principles and performance domains.
